https://github.com/duckduckgo/Android.git,07c7cdb32977ceaff1139008cdeafb79383cf430,"Add latest bad health state to the breakage reports (#1654)
We added the runtime bad health detection to internal builds and we have been testing the concept internally, resulting in something valuable. So we want to promote it to production.

Up until now, the health detection was triggering a notification whenever a bad health was detected in AppTP. The (internal) user could click through the notification to get to the diagnostics screen where the information about the AppTP health is displayed.
The diagnostics screen also contains a CTA to send a pixel with the health information.
Some things that we observe during the time we ran this in internal builds:
* memory metrics were too sensitive and the majority of the cases resulted in false possitive
* all other metrics (related to network queues and tracer packets) spot on and every time that triggered they caught a real bad health state
As we move the runtime health detection to production, this PR:
* enables the runtine health monitoring in production builds
* lowers the sensitivity of the memory metrics
* when the user sends an app breakage report, IF the system is in bad health, we will also include in the report the relevant metrics
* the data included in the reports does not contain any private information
* the data included in the reports are never static so that different reports can't be connected
* the low memory event has to be sustain for 15min for it to trigger a bad health alert
* we have added the capability to `redact` any raw metric that may present a privacy concern so that they are not sent
* added a `AppHealthCallback` that can be implemented for anybody that wants to get system health updates. Two components implement such public API:
* `AppHealthCallbackNotification` -> only for internal builds that will trigger a notification upon bad health
* `AppBadHealthStateHandler` -> for all builds that will store the latest bad health state or clear the store when the system is in good health
* added a `AppHealthStateCollector` that will be called when submitting the app breakage report
* will append the bad health data to the breakage report **only** if the system is currently in bad health

- [x] use [this website](https://onlinestringtools.com/convert-base64-to-string) to decode the report and verify only the `fake` metrics are added to the report and that the only alert is the `tunReadAlerts`

This stack of PRs will offer users a way of saying to never ask to save a password for this site again.
This PR updates the `Save Password` prompt to include a `Never Ask for This Site` option:
- If the user dismisses the dialog using the ✖️ button, the back button or by tapping outside of the dialog, then we treat that as a ""not now"" action (same as before this PR)
- If the user taps on `Never Ask for This Site` button, we securely store the e-tld+1 for the site so that we won't prompt them to save a password for this site again

In addition, we update the password management screen (`Settings->Passwords`) such that if we have >= 0 never saved sites, then we add to the overflow menu to allow the user to reset the list. We do not provide any way for the user to see the list of sites that are excluded or to individually delete them; it's a global reset only.
## Resetting never saved list from management screen
#### Never save prompt, dismissing using the various ""not now"" options
- [ ] Enter username and password (>= 4 characters) and tap Login button
- [ ] Dismiss the dialog using ✖️ button. repeat steps to visit site and enter details. Verify the prompt shows again.
- [ ] Dismiss the dialog using back button. repeat steps to visit site and enter details. Verify the prompt shows again.
- [ ] Dismiss the dialog by tapping outside the dialog. repeat steps to visit site and enter details. Verify the prompt shows again.
#### Never save prompt, choosing ""never again"" option
- [ ] Enter username and password (>= 4 characters) and tap Login button
- [ ] Tap on the `Never Ask for This Site` button
- [ ] Go back and refresh the page. Enter username and password (>= 4 characters) and tap Login button.
- [ ] Verify there is no prompt to save again for this site.

column1: The CL adds ComplexLayout, a type of measure box which composes its
children during its own composition. Using this measure box, the entire
tree of component nodes will be built as a result of AndroidCraneView
being emitted, without waiting for subsequent collect calls. Avoiding
starting one subcomposition per measure box should be a performance
improvement. ComplexLayout does not provide collect, but expects
children to be passed when creating/emitting it, which means, among
- it is no longer possible to collect a composable that depends on
- it is no longer possible to collect multiple composables
The above cases should be supported, but this will come in a further CL.
Therefore, the current CL leaves in place (but deprecates) the already
existing ComplexMeasureBox (and related classes). The implementation
supports combined hierarchies of ComplexMeasureBox and ComplexLayout.
The CL adds Layout, which is a ComplexLayout unaware of intrinsic
Example usage for a Row widget:
Test: ran SingleCompositionLayoutTest and layout tests

,EnergySmell,qwen3.5:122b,HighComplexityDebt,The commit refactors the layout system by replacing ComplexMeasureBox with a new ComplexLayout implementation to reduce composition overhead. It removes deprecated classes, simplifies inheritance hierarchies (e.g., removing InternalMeasurable interface), and consolidates logic into more maintainable structures like MeasurableImpl. This reduces technical debt associated with supporting dual measure box systems.",,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,

The PR adds a code generator for (remote) feature flags.
For instance, the following remote feature:
can be represented with the following code:
* the `MyRemoteFeature` will be the class used to check the state of the flags
* the `MyRemoteFeature` shall be annotated with `FeatureName` that will specify the name of the global feature
* the `MyRemoteFeature` shall be annotated with `ContributesRemoteFeature` for the code to be generated and shall specify
* _NOTE: settings and exceptions store are optional. If not provided, neither will be stored_
* the `MyRemoteFeature` class shall contain one method called `self()`
* this method will return the state of the global `featureName`
* the `MyRemoteFeature` may content other methods, dubbed after the sub-feature name that will also return `Toggle`
The codegen adds validation checks to ensure the correctness of the feature interfaces
After, to use the feature one can do the following:
The magic is done in two steps:
* `ContributesRemoteFeatureCodeGenerator` class is triggered by interfaces annotated with `ContributesRemoteFeature` that represent the feature.
* generates and contributes a `PrivacyFeaturePlugin` that will do the proper parsing for the feature
* generates a dynamic proxy for the feature flag interface. This proxy intercepts all interface function calls and then checks the state of the toggles in the `Toggle.Store`
* finally, the proxy implementation leaves in `FeatureToggles` class

https://github.com/androidx/androidx.git,dc0a1d4b9a92fae2a35762c03f0a75f40850f00b,"Consolidate all failure messaging with Fact + Step in FailureMetadata
NOTE TO API COUNCIL: Please ignore this CL, API tracking is only enabled
for Kruth to compare compatibility with Google Truth, and this is an
unpublished library with no intention to publish.
A required change to support the public API that Google Truth uses to
allow users to build custom assertions on custom subjects AND still
allow Truth to provide a way to do soft asserts, including its custom
JUnit rules which allow test assertions against custom truth assertions.
This change consolidates all the possible methods of throwing an
AssertionError into one place from FailureMetadata that is overridable
via custom FailureStrategy, with the failure message generated by a List
of Fact, messagesToPrepend (existing Kruth placeholder) and ""Step""s
(internal class supporting builder api for the assertion chain). The
builders around Step allows Truth to differentiate between root subject
and the child subject in more complex assertion chains (e.g., an
IterableSubject generated from the keys of a MapSubject).
Now that we have the same error messaging architecture as Truth, we can
also fix many of the placeholder messages so that they are exactly what
Truth would throw, which is important for compatibility with users who
use the Expect / ExpectFailure API to assert the error messages of
custom subjects, which may inherit or wrap existing subjects provided by
One bad thing is that this introduces a lot of wrapping classes around
messages and considering that Lazy delegation for message creation was
left out, there's a lot we can improve in terms of performance (inline
value class, lazy delegate, etc), but left this for a later CL.
One more thing that was not cleaned up as part of this change is the
numerous fail methods in Subject, which essentially all call into each
other and have varying levels of visibility to support inline reified
and either converting package-private Truth APIs to internal or
protected, since package-private doesn't exist in Kotlin.
This CL also removes data class from FailureMetadata, which is not
recommended anymore for public API due to binary compat guarantees.
Note: This depends on a small part of Guava for lenient String
formatting which was ported into KMP here.
Note: Many of the error messages are still not quite exactly correct,
but fixing them all in one change will make it too difficult to review
this, so left it for a later CL. To be clear this CL does not make
things worse, just doesn't finish fixing all the cases.
Note: Additional tests for Facts + Step depend on ExpectFailure which
has been split into a dependent CL for reviewability.

,EnergySmell,qwen3.5:122b,HighComplexityDebt,The commit refactors FailureMetadata to consolidate failure messaging logic, removing complex nested methods and data classes while simplifying message formatting. This reduces code complexity by eliminating redundant Fact construction patterns and streamlining the assertion chain handling.",,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,

- Introduced a base class for fragments (`AnkiFragment`) with a `@param layout` documentation referencing `CardViewerFragment`. This base class consolidates common functionality and methods for all future fragments by inheriting `AnkiFragment`.
- Extracted methods from NoteEditor to AnkiFragment for potential reuse in other fragments.
- Implemented `OnMenuItemClickListener` to handle toolbar menu item clicks.
- Initialized Onboarding after the view is created to prevent initialization errors.

- Split `onCreate()` into `onCreate()` and `onViewCreated()` to accommodate differences between activities and fragments in Android. In fragments, the view hierarchy setup (`onViewCreated()`) occurs separately from initialization (`onCreate()`), ensuring operations requiring access to the fragment's view are correctly handled post-creation.
- Replaced `IntentCompat` with `BundleCompat` to handle null data scenarios.
- Utilized `Bundle` instead of `Intent` in `openNewNoteEditor()` to avoid null data issues with `intent.extras`.
- Updated `SingleFragmentActivity.getIntent()` to support optional `intentAction` parameters, enabling dynamic intent actions like sharing text, images, and processing text.
- Extract common initialization from IntentHandler and IntentHandler2 into AbstractIntentHandler.
- Resolved error triggering when opening NoteEditor from a shortcut (`Long press AnkiDroid App -> Add`) by redirecting the intent target from NoteEditor to IntentHandler2.
- Removed NoteEditor from the manifest file due to its transition from an Activity to a Fragment.
- Moved `NoteEditor` specific intent filters from `NoteEditor` to `IntentHandler2`
- Updated test files under the guidance of David in discord channel and feedback to ensure comprehensive test coverage aligns with the updated architecture and intent handling in AnkiDroid.

- CardBrowserTest: The addIntent returns both SingleFragmentActivity.FRAGMENT_NAME_EXTRA and SingleFragmentActivity.FRAGMENT_ARGS_EXTRA. Since NoteEditor.EXTRA_DID is located within SingleFragmentActivity.FRAGMENT_ARGS_EXTRA, the assertions needed to be adjusted to extract and verify the arguments bundle directly.
This commit refactors NoteEditor into a Fragment, improves intent handling across activities, and enhances fragment lifecycle management for consistent behavior and performance in AnkiDroid.

By performing the essential migration inside the CollectionManager
queue, we ensure that it has exclusive access to the collection files
for the duration of the call. This should hold even for legacy code, as
CollectionHelper.getCol() blocks until the queue is free.
I found the current code somewhat hard to follow, due in large part to
its Java origins, so I've also tried to tidy it up a fair bit. I think one
of the main reasons scoped storage has stalled so long is that people were
a bit intimidated by the code (I was at least!), and I hope that by making
it a bit simpler, people will feel more comfortable jumping in and helping
to get it over the line.
- The validation and copying steps were intermixed and spread through
multiple files, which made it hard to follow. The source/dest folders are
now determined/validated separately, and then the actual copying
is done in a CollectionManager method. The chain starts in deckpicker.kt:migrate(),
- The typealiases and newtypes have been removed, as I felt they made
the code harder to follow. The newtype also served to validate the folder
was a scoped storage folder, but that's now done in our validation routine.
Instead, the variable names have been renamed to be clearer, eg
- -wal are now copied if found. Usually they would get
removed when the collection is closed, but if there is a stale -wal file,
SQLite won't clean it up until a write action is performed on the DB, so it's
- The corruption check before and after migration has been removed. For one,
it was only checking some referential integrity issues, and not actually checking
if the SQLite DB was corrupt. We could change the code to fix this, but I don't
see the point - we're already confirming the bytes of the copied files exactly match
the bytes of the source files, so the extra checks seem superfluous. Doing a full
integrity check on large collections is also quite slow, and risks hanging the
UI if legacy code happens to try access the collection.
- Removed the CollectionHelper code that was opening another instance of the backend.
- Removed the LockedCollection machinery, as CollectionManager takes its place.
- Removed the RetriableException code, as it only existed to deal with concurrent
access that can not happen with CollectionManager.
Tentatively closes #13870. The first report looked like migration being
launched twice in parallel, and the second appears to have been caused by
StudyOptionsFragment refreshing the screen (perhaps in onResume),

,StorageBloatSmell,qwen3.5:122b,ConcurrencySmell,The commit explicitly addresses race conditions and concurrency issues during file migration. It introduces a queue-based mechanism via CollectionManager to ensure exclusive access to collection files, adds locking mechanisms (LockedCollection) to prevent concurrent opening/corruption, and implements retry logic for race conditions between closing and locking the collection.",,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,

column1: This CL does a few things:
The current API can lead to false positives. The Compose
""KeyEvent"" has a ""Key"" property. If we don't have a
matching ""Key"" value, (i.e. the platform sends us an
unknown key), we currently use Key.Unknown to represent
it. This has the drawback that two different unknown
keys map to the same value. For example, if a platform
does not have Key.Home, then the condition
will pass for any unknown key. The root cause is that
""Key"" is a property, and it has to be set even if it
is not supported on a particular platform. This CL
Now the different platforms are not forced to populate
the property with some default value but just have to
return false when the key is unknown.
2. Ability to distinguish between left and right Alt keys
We Currently use an integer ""keycode"" to distinguish between
different keys. This works fine for Android because every
key has a unique keycode. The desktop however, uses the same
keycode for left and right ""Alt"" keys and uses an additional
property called ""Location"" to distinguish between the left
and right keys. The result is that the left and right Alt
Keys are identical in Compose. Thr root cause is that the
Key class is an inline class (Integer) that forces the
desktop to use an integer to represent each key. Now that
we changed the api to keyEvent.isKey(), the desktop can
provide an implementation that is backed by multiple
3. Exposes Native Android and Desktop Key Events
When the API provided with the Compose KeyEvent is not
sufficient, the user should be allowed to access the native
KeyEvent (as an escape hatch). This CL enables that by
adding a property called ""nativeKey"" which exposes the native
Relnote: ""The native keyEvent can now be accessed through keyEvent.nativeKeyEvent""

,SecurityVulnerability,qwen3.5:122b,HighComplexityDebt,The commit refactors the KeyEvent API to resolve false positives and platform inconsistencies by replacing a property-based approach with an `isKey()` method, simplifying key identification logic across Android and Desktop platforms. It also removes complex bit-packing/unpacking logic for key codes (replacing `Long` with `Int`) and eliminates redundant helper functions, reducing technical debt in the codebase.",,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,

This CL adds a new parameter to clickable and combinedClickable, lazilyCreateIndication, and changes the interactionSource parameter type to be nullable. When lazilyCreateIndication is `true` (by default if interactionSource is null, and indication is an IndicationNodeFactory), an internal MutableInteractionSource will be lazily created along with the indication instance, delaying work until there is an incoming Interaction. This reduces the amount of work that needs to be done during composition - if the clickable never produces an interaction (it is never focused / hovered / pressed), then the indication instance never needs to be created.
For components that observe the provided InteractionSource, but never emit events, they can explicitly pass `true` to lazilyCreateIndication to opt in to the same behavior.

To take advantage of this change, higher level components should change from remembering a default InteractionSource to defaulting to null in their API signature. Components that explicitly observe a hoisted InteractionSource should consider providing `true` to lazilyCreateIndication if the InteractionSource is a) created internally by the component b) only observed, and never emitted to.

Relnote: ""Clickable and combinedClickable's interactionSource parameter has been made nullable, and a new parameter has been added: lazilyCreateIndication. When lazilyCreateIndication is `true` (by default if interactionSource is null, and indication is an IndicationNodeFactory), an internal MutableInteractionSource will be lazily created along with the indication instance, delaying work until there is an incoming Interaction. To take advantage of this change, it is recommended that you:
1. Migrate away from the deprecated Indication#rememberUpdatedInstance API if you haven't already, as this optimization is only possible for IndicationNodeFactory implementations

2. Explicitly pass indication to clickable and pass null for interactionSource if you aren't observing it yourself. If you are observing the interactionSource, but you are never emitting an Interaction, you can explicitly set `true` for lazilyCreateIndication.

3. Prefer the clickable overload with a required MutableInteractionSource and Indication parameter, and explicitly pass LocalIndication.current if you are calling this modifier only within composition, as this is more performant than the other clickable overload which needs to use Modifier.composed to retrieve LocalIndication.""

,EnergySmell,qwen3.5:122b,EnergySmell,The commit introduces a 'lazilyCreateIndication' parameter to defer the creation of indication instances until an interaction occurs. This optimization reduces unnecessary work during composition for clickable components that never receive interactions, directly addressing CPU consumption and performance efficiency.",,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,
Re-key was not working as expected because when we restart the tunnel we always re-register.
Note: This PR also reduces the amount of models to represent the WG configuration to basically one.
There are now two interfaces related to the tunnel, ie `WgTunnel` and `WgTunnelConfig`
* `WgTunnel` exposes APIs to create the tunnel config and (optionally) save it
* `WgTunnelConfig` allows to read the saved tunnel config and also clear it.
This is mainly done because the UI (browser process) needs read access to tunnel config, while the VPN process is the one that needs access to create the tunnel.

This PR is an attempt at addressing those issues. It introduces
a `withCol` function that is intended to be used instead of
CollectionHelper.getCol(). For example, code that previously looked
Can now be used like this:
The block is run on a background thread, and other withCol calls made
in parallel will be queued up and executed sequentially. Because of
the exclusive access, routines can safely close and reopen the
collection inside the block without fear of affecting other callers.
It's not practical to update all the legacy code to use withCol
immediately - too much work is required, and coroutines are not
accessible from Java code. The intention here is that this new path is
gradually bought into. Legacy code can continue calling CollectionHelper.
getCol(), which internally delegates to CollectionManager.
Two caveats to be aware of:
- Legacy callers will wait for other pending operations to complete
before they receive the collection handle, but because they retain it,
subsequent access is not guaranteed to be exclusive.
- Because getCol() and colIsOpen() are often used on the main thread,
they will block the UI if a background operation is already running.
Logging has been added to help diagnose this, eg messages like:
- added TR function for accessing translations without a col reference
- onCreateOptionsMenu() needed refactoring to avoid blocking the UI.
- The blocking colIsOpen() call in onPrepareOptionsMenu() had to be

- The subscribers in ChangeManager need to be cleared out at the start

After trying to setup tests for jetnews I identified several issues in
our synchonization. After fixing these several other issues surfaced
that were previously hidden by incorrect sync.
I have introduce ComposeIdlingResource that registers itself into
Espresso. This way we leave synchronization to Espresso. It removed
several flakes we had because we used to wait for idle compose and then
did the ""Espresso wait"". From now on Espresso does all of it at the same
time. This helps in cases where we have regular Android event that later
Thanks to us having ComposeIdlingResource we no longer need to wait
after we perform actions (doClick) for idle Compose. It also makes tests
faster as we don't sync when not needed. This unfortunately broke tests
that were relying on this fact (incorrectly) and touching the UI thread
So far lots of tests were using waitForIdleCompose() and touching
variables from different threads or did not use any kind of
synchonization at all. They were lucky because methods like doClick used
to wait for UI being idle before leaving. To address that I have
introduced runOnIdleCompose that waits for idle compose and performs the
given lambda on the UI thread. This makes assertions nice and stable! :)
I have also removed waitForIdle {} as it would encourage bad practices
Other thing discovered after I removed the flakes was that we were
always recreating SemanticsConfiguration which previsouly wasn't
detected. This however was something on which the testing API relied on.
So I memoized the SemanticsConfiguration. This will need to work with
We were also not disposing compositions between tests. This lead to more
synchronization issues as old compositions were still part of the app.
Another wrong thing was that we always send any events to the first
ComposeView. This was wrong in case the second ComposeView would need to
work with events. It wasn't detected becase  MultipleComposeRootsTest
had wrong assertions. Now it is fixed.

This change is a series of improvement to path APIs, in large part as
foundational work for upcoming changes to Cubic/PathEasing:
- Add the ability to set the winding direction when adding shapes to
a Path (addOval(), addRect() and addRoundRect()). The winding
direction is necessary to create complex shapes with holes. For
instance a donut is made of two concentric circles each with a
favor of quadraticTo() and relativeQuadraticTo(). In addition to
being shorter names, these APIs are also more consistent with
- Add APIs to iterate over the segments of a Path using Path.iterate().
The API is modeled after androidx-graphics:graphics-path. A key
component of the API is the ability, turned on by default, to convert
conic path segments into quadratic path segments.
The new PathIterator API provides the ability to iterate over the
- The ""OOP"" way with allocations, by returning a new PathSegment
- The ""graphics"" way by passing a float array that gets filled with
the data required to describe the path segment.
The first API is useful for readability/ease of use, while the second
one is necessary for performance/to avoid allocations.
To make this feature possible across all supported API levels, the
compose-ui-graphics module now depends on graphics-path.
Note: this CL provides a Skiko implementation of path iteration albeit
with a limitation. The conics to quadratics part of the implementation
is missing because Skiko currently contains a bug preventing this
feature from being implemented properly. See the issue on GitHub for
This CL also does a bit of cleanup throughout related code.
RelNote: Added Path.iterate() to iterate over Path segments
RelNote: Use Path.quadraticTo() instead of Path.quadraticBezierTo()
RelNote: You can now set the winding direction when calling Path.addOval(), addRect(), and addRoundRect()

https://github.com/androidx/androidx.git,62fd13d2f27294182f096a186ea4e0814421e485,"Add ability to customize LazyLayout prefetching strategy

Our goal is to enable testing of different prefetching strategies, including what to prefetch and when that work is scheduled. This diff refactors the existing logic behind a strategy pattern, maintaining the current choices of how much/when as the default strategy.
The existing logic is split between LazyListState (+ LazyGridState, etc), which decides *what* to prefetch, and LazyLayoutPrefetcher, which decides *when* to execute it.
- LazyList accepts an optional LazyListPrefetchStrategy falling back to DefaultLazyListPrefetchStrategy which uses the existing logic.
- LazyListPrefetchStrategy contains LazyList specific callbacks that a strategy can use to decide how to manage prefetching (currently onScroll and onVisibleItemsChanged)

- LazyListPrefetchStrategy extends the LazyLayoutPrefetchStrategy interface, which allows LazyLayout to connect a PrefetchContext to the strategy for it to execute prefetches. This is analogous to how LazyLayout connects the LazyLayoutPrefetcher to a LazyLayoutPrefetchState before this diff.
- PrefetchContext gives access to the precompose API from SubcomposeLayout instead of exposing how to scheduling a prefetch - this was done to allow a strategy to determine its own scheduling logic
- LazyLayoutPrefetcher mostly stays as is, except it's given a PrefetchContext instead of setting/unsetting itself in onRemembered/onForgotten and is directly used from the default prefetch strategy.
I didn't try to implement correct logic for anything besides LazyList and didn't clean up LazyLayoutPrefetchState, just to get feedback on this approach first.

I also uploaded https://android-review.googlesource.com/c/platform/frameworks/support/+/2817620 as an example of custom logic using the API to change the amount to prefetch and when to execute prefetch.
Test: * Rotate sample app with custom PrefetchStrategy. LazyList still uses chosen PrefetchStrategy after rotation.
* Run TrivialListScrollBenchmark without custom strategy, see no changes
* Run TrivialListScrollBenchmark with custom strategy/executor, see potential perf wins when rows can take over a frame to execute.
Relnote: Adds an experimental API for configuring prefetch behavior of LazyLists.

Taking a cue from CoroutineContext, a Modifier is a linear chain of
specialized elements that provide additional decoration/context for
a UI component. Modifiers serve two design purposes for Compose UI:
The first is to address the limitation we have in composable
functions where it is impractical to statically determine whether
a call to another composable function will emit zero, one, or many
layout nodes. This affects the API design of composables intended to
We could choose to tie into Kotlin's contracts system for this or
otherwise lean on the Compose compiler for additional analysis, but this
brings along significant unavoidable complexity for new developers along
with new considerations for the tooling ecosystem, included but not
limited to the editing experience, code review tools and API
We'd prefer not to create more new concepts at that low of a level
unless the reward for that tradeoff is overwhelming. It's not clear that
the calling syntax enabled by such a change would reach that bar.
Which leads to the second concern: the API ergonomics of single-child
composables. The existing API surface of composables such as Padding
impose a down and to the right march of indentation in source code that
impacts readability. Android developers are used to expressing
decorations such as padding, backgrounds or LayoutParams values as
properties on the affected XML element itself rather than as wrappers.
At the same time we want to retain the composability and extensibility
of the system and not end up with a repeat of android.widget.View, where
a fixed framework superclass becomes host to dozens of cross-cutting but
At a glance, Modifiers as implemented in this proof of concept seem to
address these issues. This change implements modification of measurement
and layout, but the idea could be extended to also support drawing,
hit testing, input handling, semantics, arbitrary LayoutParams-style
arguments to parent layouts and more. The concatenation style also
supports partial compositions of Modifiers to be fed through many layers
of the hierarchy, permitting style-like usages and overlay/override

- Removing all pools except for the primary tracing packet array

https://github.com/androidx/androidx.git,f958564f327d27b02f262d56fa3ebb29fd04ad05,"Do not track remote states locally within PageFetcher for PageEvents
Previously from fetcher side, we locally tracked remote load states
per-generation to inject them into the page event stream, however this
leads to issues between generations, where the local state is incorrect
before it receives an event from either the source or remote stream.
This CL changes .injectRemoteEvents into a custom .combine operator so
that we always wait for a real event from both remote and source flows,
so that we are guaranteed to have valid states for both. We also now
immediately send REFRESH=Loading from source so that it is less likely
to drop remote events due to waiting on source flow from .combine.
The reason we use a custom .combine operator is to remove the ""batching""
logic in .combine, which would normally output the latest values from
each Flow and can miss emissiosn when we get multiple emissions on the
same Flow, as opposed to alternating emissions.
Since it also affected some tests, this CL also fixes a longstanding
issue with cancellation on invalidation. Previously, a collector
would continue to receive remote events after invalidation, which
can make it difficult for Java users or those not using collectLatest to
switch to the next generation, since submitData would never return until
cancelled. We now eagerly cancel collection on the remote stream (within
a generation) to close the Flow<PageEvent> from a PagingData as soon as
RelNote: ""LoadStates from Paging now await valid values from both
PagingSource and RemoteMediator before emitting downstream between
generations. This prevents new generations of PagingData from sending
NotLoading in CombinedLoadStates.source.refresh if it was already
Loading; new generations of PagingData will now always correctly begin
with Loading for refresh state instead of first resetting to NotLoading
Cancellation on past generations now happen eagerly on invalidation /
new generations. It should no longer be required to use .collectLatest
on Flow<PagingData>, although it is still highly recommended to do so.""

https://github.com/androidx/androidx.git,3f41fb1c143630b3b06451e4454924a127dbe5c4,"Proper splitting and theming of Material surfaces and Inks
Big refactoring for Material and Inks and renaming to be in sync the latest
3) clip (should be moved out of the material module later)
1) Now we have RippleSurface - the surface where the ripples will be drawn.
2) RippleEffect - the base class for ripple animations.
3) RippleEffectFactory - the factory interface to create ripple effects like DefaultRippleEffect and all further implementations we would have.
4) RippleTheme - the holder for the current styles with an Ambient for getting this and a default style based on the MaterialTheme.
5) Ripple(old InkResponse) doesn't take onTap callbacks as a parameter anymore as we don't need it with our PointerInput system. The tap will be handled by a descendant gesture detector.
1) Surface - the main class which represents a surface in Material Design. It is quite small component which just combines all the necessary logic like:
a) SurfaceMeasureBox - reserves the space(simple measure box which just position the children in the left top corner and takes all the available space
b) CachingClipper - helps to cache the clipper provided clip to not recreate it on every recomposing

c) DrawShadow - draws a shadow for the surface (didn't work now if you provide not 0 elevation as it is not implemented on a graphic side) (there is an ambient for configuring the shadow color - CurrentShadowColor)
d) ClipPath - clips the canvas with the surface's shape
e) DrawColor - just fills the background into a provided color(use MaterialColors.surface by default)
f) RippleSurface - a surface on top of which we draw ripple effects
g) DrawBorder - draws a border for the provided shape
2) TransparentSurface - a simple composable to create a Surface without a color and elevation.
3) Card - a simple composable to create a Surface with a default surface background color from MaterialColors.
Test: Ripple activity still works and now uses colors from the MaterialTheme

During a suspending transaction Room uses a thread from the transaction executor, starts an event loop in it and dispatches suspending database operations to it so they are all encapsulated within the transaction coroutine. It is usually expected that the transaction thread is different from the one starting the transaction, but in some cases they are the same. To handle such reentrant cases the `withTransaction()` has been refactored to no longer rely joining on a control job and instead it will execute the suspending transaction block from within the `runBlocking` in the transaction thread.

Executing the suspending transaction block in the `runBlocking` prevents a deadlock situation due to the continuation bouncing between the caller to the transaction thread and back. The deadlock specifically happened when Room started the event loop via `runBlocking`, resumed the `acquireTransactionThread()` coroutine but also joined the `runBlocking` coroutine with the control job. These set of events would cause a deadlock when both the acquire thread and transaction thread are the same because `acquireTransactionThread()` is unable to continue as it is waiting for `runBlocking` to finish which in turns waits for the control job to be done. Meanwhile the job never completes because the database transaction does not finish, i.e. `TransactionElement.release` is never called. By eliminating the need to wait on a control job but still support nested transactions via the transaction context, Room is able to avoid the deadlock situation.

This behaviour was mostly observed in tests where the transaction executor is usually an instant executor, but it could happen in an actual application if Room is configured to share a thread pool executor as the transaction executor and a background coroutine dispatcher and the thread pool is sufficiently busy that only one thread is left for both starting the transaction (to acquire the transaction thread) and the transaction thread itself.

,ConcurrencySmell,qwen3.5:122b,ConcurrencySmell,The commit explicitly addresses a deadlock situation caused by reentrant transaction threads. The message describes how `acquireTransactionThread()` and `runBlocking` interactions could cause a deadlock when the caller thread and transaction thread are identical, which is a classic concurrency issue involving coroutine scopes and thread management.",,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,

Delay and delegates it to the underlying dispatcher, just like
This CL also adds a callback that runs after each frame in
TestMonotonicFrameClock. This change was originally in aosp/2121435.
This allows us to perform measure and layout after each frame if
necessary, which aligns the measure/layout timing with production.

https://github.com/androidx/androidx.git,c3c96aef1d6c366ee1658cd8dfa27588231740c2,"Deprecates rememberRipple and RippleTheme, adds createRippleNode API
This CL adds a new ripple implementation (createRippleNode) built using Modifier.Node APIs, so it can be used inside IndicationNodeFactory.
This leads to large performance improvements and greatly reduced allocations.
IndicationNodeFactory does not support calling Composable functions, so RippleTheme cannot be used to theme ripples built using the new node
APIs. In addition, having one singular library as the source of truth for ripple theme leads to tension and problems between design systems:
for example the default RippleTheme functions are actually the material spec, not material3, so they should have been versioned with the
material library instead. Similarly, if you are wrapping material / material3 with your own theme, both themes will end up 'fighting' over the
RippleTheme - the theme configuration is a property of the design system, not a generic system.
As a result, this CL deprecates rememberRipple and RippleTheme - the intention moving forward is for design system libraries to provide their
own theming APIs for ripples (if desired), and their own ripple Indication implementations that consume their own theme values. This allows us
to simplify the layering and API shape, and allows for more flexibility between custom design systems. createRippleNode encapsulates all of
the ripple logic so design system implementations can just delegate to createRippleNode after querying their theme values in a separate node.

Relnote: ""Deprecates rememberRipple, RippleTheme and LocalRippleTheme, adds createRippleNode. Instead of using these APIs, use the new ripple Indication and theming APIs in design system libraries such as material / material3. See developer.android.com for migration / backwards compatibility information and more background information behind this change.""

,EnergySmell,qwen3.5:122b,EnergySmell,The commit explicitly states that the new ripple implementation built using Modifier.Node APIs leads to 'large performance improvements and greatly reduced allocations'. The diff shows a migration from Composable-based ripples (rememberRipple) to Node-based ripples (createRippleNode), which allows rendering on the render thread away from the main UI thread, reducing CPU usage and improving battery life during animations.",,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,

* Support emit perfetto traces on Android and JVM.
* `0` allocations when emitting a trace section.
* Use `mutableTypes` in Wire, and expose platform specific storage sinks.
* Make it trivial to support other platforms (like Darwin).
* `reference` emits no trace sections (even when tracing is turned on.). This is just purely work.
* `platformTracing` = reference + emit trace sections using the `androidx.tracing` API.
The trace packets go through their full lifecycle (including recycling).
The only thing we don't do is to encode the packets into the proto stream.
* `customTracingDisabled` emits no trace sections given tracing is disabled.
* `customTracingInMemorySink` = reference + actually encodes the packets into an in-memory proto stream.
* Note: Trace packet serialization costs are not fully accounted for in the `platformTracing` benchmark.
The actual serialization happens outside the perview of the benchmark.
* `NoOpSink` is unsurprisingly faster than the platform version (that emits trace sections).
* Tweaking the `bufferSize` on the Okio `Sink` does not make any difference to the benchmark.
Test: Added some JVM tests and tracing overhead benchmarks.
Relnote: Initial commit for Tracing Driver (experimental)

column1: This CL creates APIs that allow a developer to create a DataStore that can be used during direct boot mode, while guarding against any attempt to utilize the existing APIs during direct boot mode (where possible).
There are 2 main paths in creating a DataStore in the current existing API. These are:
[2] Through a DataStoreDelegate `dataStore()` (which eventually delegates to Pathway [1] via a new DataStoreFile creation).
This CL creates 2 new API paths reserved only for use during direct boot mode. These are:
[2] Through a DataStoreDelegate `deviceProtectedDataStore()` (which eventually delegates to Pathway [1] via a new DeviceProtectedDataStoreFile creation).

As directBoot support requires the presence of a DeviceProtectedStorageContext in order to create the DataStore file, this change introduces APIs that can access the context and use it for direct boot mode after verification. Direct Boot mode APIs require a minimum of SDK level 24, therefore these APIs are restricted to this API level.

https://github.com/androidx/androidx.git,441e46a0dc02b69de2ea411e913abe4fad328e5a,"Refactor Room Gradle Plugin for lazy configuration

This CL updates the RoomGradlePlugin to adjust for lazy configuration, including schema configurations. There are 3 mayor changes in this CL that all together make the Room Gradle Plugin better work with lazy configurations.

1. The schema configurations are now stored on a DomainObjectSet which enables the plugin to act as schema configuration are added without relying on the timing on how other plugins are configured, i.e. avoid bad patterns like afterEvaluate() or expecting all RoomExtension functions to be called in some withPlugin() lambda. The APIs where also updated to be more Directory centric instead of string based resolving b/279748243.

2. The plugin was incorrectly using tasks.withType() with a lambda that resolved in eager task configuration. This CL updates the plugin to use configureEach which is lazy. However, due to configuring tasks within a configureEach is not permitted (https://github.com/gradle/gradle/issues/27030) the RoomSchemaCopyTask are registered as soon as a user provides a schema configuration and the task itself is then wired when a schema configuration is matched with a variant / target.

3. Updated the schema configuration matching priority to adjust to the laziness of user adding configurations. Specifically the plugin must account that if a user provides a schema configuration that matches all variants and then provides a more specific one (higher priority) the plugin must adjust the registered tasks (see added test testFlavoredProjectPriority() for validation). This is done by storing matches in the CommonIntegration container and later retrieving them when tasks are actually configured.
Test: Existing + manual in sample project

Various changes according to ongoing API reviews. The major
change is a refactor which splits out the state logic of JankStats
PerformanceMetricsState is a per-window singleton (as JankStats
allows storage and retrieval of app state in a single object within
the hierarchy. Meanwhile, JankStats is now a regular object (not a
singleton), created via constructor, that is wholly managed by the
application. This division should make it clearer and cleaner such
that logic outside of the app code (e.g., RecyclerView) can retrieve
the PerformanceMetricsState and store information there without needing to
deal with (and potentially interfere with) JankStats.
Note that PerformanceMetricsState could even be used by other non-JankStats
libraries eventually; it is not specific to JankStats (though it still
contains frame timing logic, so it is best used by performance clients).
Also cleaned up other APIs and functionality:
FrameMetrics usaged protected against doubled reports.
JankStats now takes a Window directly. PerformanceMetricsState takes a view
in the hierarchy, similar to the previous JankStats singleton.
PerformanceMetricsState is cached inside a MetricsStateHolder object.
Clients should request a holder and can then query it quickly to
get the state, rather than having to call the instance getter each time,
which walks the view hierarchy up to the root.

https://github.com/androidx/androidx.git,96d8d4d043efa7ab5041ca883b25fbf1821f8edc,"[GH] [Room][Compiler Processing] Support getting non type elements

Currently the Room compiler processing abstraction is set up to use the pattern of processing steps, including using auto-common for javac processing. Our existing processors (and I assume many other people's) do not use this approach and I'd prefer not to have to convert to that pattern. While annotated elements can be looked up directly with XRoundEnv, that currently only works for type elements.

This PR adds to XRoundEnv a new function to look up annotated elements of all kinds, not just type elements. This allows processors more flexibility in how they use the compiler processing (and mirrors the patterns we use with javac processing in our libraries).
The implementation is fairly straightforward, I just created new functions to wrap each possible type of Element and Symbol, leveraging existing classes.
I believe this could benefit from caching, but that could be added in the future.
I also did not attempt to modify XProcessingStep to support elements of all kinds, since the pattern it enforces seems directed towards starting with just type elements.
If this direction is OK I can also update the readme to cover this use case.

This CL aims to fundamentally address the issue stated below:
1. Currently, every use case transaction is done synchronously, meaning
that every attach/detach/reset/activate/deactivate call is handled
in their entirety, with all UseCaseCamera components and CameraGraph
created before the return of the calls (except for concurrent camera
use cases, but the API guarantees make it more manageable).
2. However, extensions requires us to get the Surfaces from
DeferrableSurfaces before initializing the session, and it cannot be
done or block the main thread. That means the use case transactions
can no longer be done synchronously.
This CL addresses this by having the following major changes:
1. Add necessary and delicate synchronization mechanisms so that we can
safely defer the creation of the new set of UseCaseCamera components.
2. Add a formal finite state machine in SessionProcessorManager so that
every call to SessionProcessor follows their correct lifetime.
The detailed mechanisms are included in comments, but the high level
1. Say, we started initializing for a new set of use cases with
2. If a new set of use cases is attached or detached, we ""close"" the
create a new one that supercedes the current session.
3. If a new use case is activated (marked as active) or detactivated,

https://github.com/androidx/androidx.git,74e9c5f21c148a8c68372c1fae290cd733dd0f46,"Refactor semantics traversal order for more accurate top bars
This change uses 1) the idea of structural significance to determine groups of nodes to visit at a time and 2) row groupings to have more accurate traversal order.

For each node examined, if it is structurally significant, then all of its children must be placed in the final order list before moving on; if the node is not, then all of its children and its children’s children are extracted, then compared with the grouping algorithm. A structurally significant node for now is determined by if it is a container (Surface), scrollable, or a collection.

This fix is mainly focused on addressing top bar ordering issues. The tests added primarily focus on different types of top bars (TopAppBars, Scaffold top bars, top bars with scrolling columns, etc.). New tests were added to `AndroidAccessibilityTest` instead of `SemanticsTests` since the new traversal order is set via ANI properties and needs the ANI provider to test.

In addition to those changes, we no longer need semantic node’s `replacedChildrenSortedByBounds` since all previous sorting has been replaced, so that function, the `sortByBounds` parameter, and the `SemanticsSort` file has been removed.

Tests in `SemanticsTests` that were related to ordering (tests that started with `testChildrenSortedByBounds`) were removed, but new tests with the same coverage were added to `AndroidAccessibilityTest` in their place.

,HighComplexityDebt,qwen3.5:122b,HighComplexityDebt,The commit refactors the semantics traversal order logic to improve accuracy, replacing a complex sorting mechanism (`sortByGeometryGroupings`, `semanticComparator`) with a new approach using structural significance and row groupings. It removes obsolete code like `replacedChildrenSortedByBounds` and associated tests while introducing new helper classes (`NodeLocationHolder`) and methods to handle layout comparisons more robustly. This is a significant refactoring aimed at reducing technical debt in the accessibility traversal logic.",,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,

1. Removed coroutine usage from CameraScope classes that was mainly used for thread confinement strategy
- This ensures that code flows run on caller thread longer and switch to background thread later in common functions when required.
2. Added thread confinement in UseCaseCameraRequestControl which serves as the entry point for capture requests to UseCaseCameraScope
- This removes the need of complicated synchronization lock handlings and thus less error-prone and reduces synchronization issues in UseCaseCameraScope layer.
3. Convenience functions are added in UseCaseThreads to better convey the purpose of the coroutine scope usages in thread confinement sites

- The primary objective in these places is thread confinement, not async operation. In future, this may be further refactored to provide thread confinement in a different way (may not even require coroutine scopes).

It is important that the synchronization points (e.g. threads.sequentialScope.launch calls) are removed from all UseCaseCameraControl classes at the same time as adding in RequestControl, so that additional sync points are not introduced in a code flow which can create hard-to-debug race conditions. TorchControl is an exception and thus carefully handled until blocking bug is resolved.

column1: https://github.com/androidx/androidx.git,ef8e9f60d94a9604380d3a00a18425f999fabcda,"Updates Material and Foundation components to expose a nullable MutableInteractionSource
Components that previously exposed an API such as:

Have been updated to instead expose a nullable MutableInteractionSource that defaults to null. This allows for some components to never create and remember a MutableInteractionSource, and allows for some other components to lazily create one only when needed. Some components always need a MutableInteractionSource currently, so if null they will just create a new one internally - so for those cases there are no changes, but it allows for future optimizations.
Also deletes long deprecated experimental Card and Surface overloads.

Relnote: ""Material and Foundation components exposing a MutableInteractionSource in their API have been updated to now expose a nullable MutableInteractionSource that defaults to null. There are no semantic changes here: passing null means that you do not wish to hoist the MutableInteractionSource, and it will be created inside the component if needed. Changing to null allows for some components to never allocate a MutableInteractionSource, and allows for other components to only lazily create an instance when they need to, which improves performance across these components. If you are not using the MutableInteractionSource you pass to these components, it is recommended that you pass null instead. It is also recommended that you make similar changes in your own components.""

,EnergySmell,qwen3.5:122b,EnergySmell,The commit explicitly states that changing MutableInteractionSource to nullable allows components to avoid creating instances when not needed, improving performance. The diff shows removal of default non-null values and internal null-checks (e.g., `interactionSource ?: remember { ... }`), which reduces unnecessary object allocations and memory usage in the UI rendering pipeline.",,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,

A) This updates the implementation details of semantics to internally

Any other usage, for now, is not expected to work.

which has been removed in favor of always doing depth-first-search.

https://github.com/androidx/androidx.git,dfbfcd90a67d66d7eab8a7381e85ae9f831cea4a,"Add experimental support for a version of Surface that handles click and make a regular Surface to block touches behind it.
Clickable Surface allows us to handle many issues at once:
1. Allows to define clicks on the Surface layout node, making a11y to be correct
2. Allows to define clicks in the single layout node without the need to transfer indication hnadling to the node below (because of the clipping inside the surface)

3. Allows for a desired default behaviour in a non-clickable Surface when we block pointer events from going under the surface. Throughout the many components it is seens like a desiged behaviour and often leads to bugs (snackbar, Drawer, modal sheet & card being norable examples of surfaces that shouldn't allow touches behind it).
To make this work properly, I refactored ModalBottomSheetLayout to work with the sheet-based swipeable (instead of content-based), since sheet is not blocking clicks.
With this one overload we solve many issues so it seems like a worthy experiment.
Relnote: BEHAVIOUR-BREAKING: Surface now consumes clicks, making clicks added via Surface(Modifier.clickable) to be a no-op. Please, use new experimental overload of Surface that accepts onClick.

Relnote: Added a new Surface overload that handles clicks as well as other clickable functionality: indication, interactionSource, enabled/disabled. It wasn't possible to use a regular non-clickable Surface with the Modifier.clickable because the Surface will not clip the ripple indication in those cases.
Test: Added new for an overload

This change is targeting to unblock implementations of Drag&Drop APIs on platforms where transfer session might be initiated only by the system itself (iOS, Web).

To do that `dragAndDropSource` now implements default start detector that not only allows delegating it to the system, but also simplifies the usage of it. Specifying custom behavior is still possible by passing a new `detectDragStart` parameter (not available on some platforms). Inside the scope of this detected there is an ability to request starting the transfer.
In addition of entry point changes in `compose:foundation`, there are important `compose:ui` API changes:
- Requesting of start transaction now limited by callback scope.
- Removing from `DragAndDrop*ModifierNode` APIs that shouldn't be exposed outside of ui module
Also these changes fix a few scenarios (even on Android):
- `DragAndDropNode.contains` check used incorrect size in case of applied padding modifier

Relnote: `Modifier.dragAndDropSource` was adopted to multiplatform - start detection is performed by Compose itself (or delegated to system in case of Web or iOS), so callback is only used for obtain a transfer data
Relnote: `DragAndDropModifierNode` is deprecated in favor to `DragAndDropSourceModifierNode` and `DragAndDropTargetModifierNode`

,SecurityVulnerability,qwen3.5:122b,HighComplexityDebt,The commit refactors the Drag&Drop API to support multiplatform, splitting `DragAndDropModifierNode` into separate source and target nodes (`DragAndDropSourceModifierNode`, `DragAndDropTargetModifierNode`) and simplifying gesture detection logic. This reduces coupling and improves code structure by separating concerns, which aligns with reducing technical debt.",,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,

The CL updates the layout protocol to permit children measuring
themselves to sizes that do not respect the constraints coming from the
parent. This enables nicer handling for cases when children need to
overflow their parent due to a minimum size they absolutely need in
order to do correct layout and drawing; it also allows more elegant
handling of alignment for children forced by their parents to be larger
than the maximum size they can occupy while doing correct layout and
When a child does not respect its constraints, the parent will not find
out about it when doing layout - the size reported by the child will be
automatically coerced inside the incoming constraints before being read
from its corresponding Placeable by the parent. Therefore, the parent
will do layout under the assumption that children will respect their
constraints. Once they assign a position to the child, this will be
automatically offset such that the child will be centered in the space
that the parent assigned to it.
The CL also adds the width, height, size, etc. modifiers, which have
priority over incoming constraints, with conflicts being resolved based
on the protocol explained above. The API shape resembles the already
existing preferredWidth, preferredHeight, etc. modifiers, which will
only affect size when this is possible under the incoming constraints.
Test: ran AndroidLayoutDrawTest, ui-layout tests, new test in LayoutSizeTest

Components that previously exposed an API such as:

Have been updated to instead expose a nullable MutableInteractionSource that defaults to null. This allows for some components to never create and remember a MutableInteractionSource, and allows for some other components to lazily create one only when needed. Some components always need a MutableInteractionSource currently, so if null they will just create a new one internally - so for those cases there are no changes, but it allows for future optimizations.

Relnote: ""tv-material components exposing a MutableInteractionSource in their API have been updated to now expose a nullable MutableInteractionSource that defaults to null. There are no semantic changes here: passing null means that you do not wish to hoist the MutableInteractionSource, and it will be created inside the component if needed. Changing to null allows for some components to never allocate a MutableInteractionSource, and allows for other components to only lazily create an instance when they need to, which improves performance across these components. If you are not using the MutableInteractionSource you pass to these components, it is recommended that you pass null instead. It is also recommended that you make similar changes in your own components.""

Add support for multiplatform KSP tasks to be configured by the Room Gradle Plugin for exporting schemas.

The code is refactored for the plugin to use 'integrations' that allow the plugin to configure various combinations of supported porjects, such as Android + Java only, Android + Kotlin, Kotlin Multiplatform (with or without Android targets), etc. This is done by using plugin ids to perform configuration and only accessing plugin classes (AGP or KGP) once the plugins have been found, an indicator that the plugin classes are in the plugin classpath.

For KMP projects, the Android target will still be configured through AGP's variant APIs as those better reflect the name of the source sets. For other Kotlin targets, then the KSP task is found with the same method as KSP's own Gradle Plugin, by inspecting the configured Kotlin targets, their compilation tasks and KSP side task.

In terms of APIs, a Kotlin target is now an acceptable 'match name' for configuring schema locations, i.e. `schemaLocation(""native"", ...)`. To improve the experience on KMP projects, per-variant schema locations will accept an 'android' prefix to align with the target name usage, i.e. `schemaLocation(""androidDemoDebug"", ...)`.
Lastly, the Room extension will now contain `generateKotlin` to enable / disable Kotlin code generation through the plugin.

,SecurityVulnerability,qwen3.5:122b,HighComplexityDebt,The commit refactors the Room Gradle Plugin to use an 'integrations' pattern, separating concerns for Android and Kotlin Multiplatform configurations. * `ViewModelProvider`, all nested types and classes with cyclic dependency have been converted to Kotlin Multiplatform.
* All methods that expects a `java.lang.Class<T>` have been converted to `expect`/`actual`, and received a new `KClass<T>` overload.

* A new constructor for `ViewModelProvider` has been added to avoid breaking binary compatibility with the Android source: the original Android source would generate overloaded constructors and a public synthetic constructor with last parameter as `DefaultConstructorMarker`, but when combining `JvmOverloads` with `expect`/`actual`, the synthetic constructor has not been generated. That is a binary compatibility.
* The `@JvmOverload` constructor has been left as an Android-only API for now.

* Kotlin Multiplatform does not support expect class with default implementation yet. Hence, the common logic has been extracted to an internal `ViewModelProviderImpl` (for stateful logic) and `ViewModelProviders` (for stateless logic).

Add a new Experimental text input composable to foundation that's specifically designed to be used as a password field.
`BasicSecureTextField` accepts 3 methods of obfuscation of its content; `Visible`, `RevealLastTyped`, and `Hidden`.
This CL also adds another Experimental API to `BasicTextField2` called `CodepointTransformation`. This new interface is targeted to be a replacement of `VisualTransformation` in new text fields.

`OffsetMapping` has been a huge source of pain for both developers and maintainers. Allowing text length to change arbitrarily has been proven to be difficult for many reasons. Writing a correct function that calculated OffsetMapping has been a very difficult task. Also, selection and text offsets do not directly translate to each other which makes selection act wonky even when `TransformedText` is calculated correctly.

`CodepointTransformation` requires a 1-to-1 mapping of each codepoint in original text, which guarantees that the length of the current input and rendered text are always the same. This makes sure that selection does not run into any weird offset misalignment.

The rewrite rules we have been using until now are easy to write but at
the end we will need a map of every single change that happened in the
support library due to the refactoring. Because of that I'm introducing
a preprocessor tool that will firstly run all the rules over the real
support libraries to build the complete map. This map is then added to
a newly generated config. This generated config will be shipped with
The libraries bytecode and XML resources rewriting was updated to use
the generated types map instead of the rules itself. That makes it
simpler and also prevents rewriting of anything that is not expected
The tool has also few new extra checks. If the preprocessor will find
any java type from support library that is not covered by any of
the given rules it will fail. That will ensure that our mapping is not
missing anything. Also the jetifier itself will fail if it finds
a type that is supposed to be rewritten but there is no rule in
the map. This will allow us to detect that we are rewriting older
versions of support libraries for instance.

The initial implementation of KeepInView logic in foundation had to use an observer to find the bounds of the focused child due to a limitation between `ui` and `foundation` layers. TextMotion comes with 2 modes; Static(default) and Animated. Static text is rendered for maximum readability and legibility on all screens from Low DPI to Ultra High DPI. Instead of special handling the AndroidSQLiteDriver and the SupportSQLiteDriver consolidate these two and any other driver that has an internal connection pool reported via the new API SQLiteDriver.hasConnectionPool to use a passthrough connection pool that does not do any connection management.
This also enables driver wrappers of AndroidSQLiteDriver as otherwise the instance check would fail leading Room to use the incorrect pool.
RoomSupportSQLite wrapper was also updated to provide compatibility for Room databases with drivers that have an internal pool, such as the AndroidSQLiteDriver.

Note that AndroidSQLiteConnection must be kept for backwards compatibility but was refactored to prepare statements of type SupportSQLiteStatement, while AndroidSQLiteStatement was merged with SupportSQLiteStatement and avoid repeated code. Moreover. in order to properly support the passthrough pool the driver handles transaction SQL statements directly.
Relnote: ""Add an API to SQLiteDriver to report if internally it has a connection pool or not.""

ReceiveContent framework consolidates 3 different input systems through a single API. It's expensive to filter out incoming items according to their media type since it requires doing at least 2 IPC calls. Therefore `Modifier.contentReceiver` does not have an enforced ""accepted media types"" parameter. However, it required a `hintMediaTypes` due to the existing contract between IMEs and the app where apps can hint to the IME what type of content that they support so that the IME can hide unsupported functionality.

This CL removes `hintMediaTypes` since a sanity check is always recommended for the received item. Even if the received content's source happens to be IME, there's still no guarantee that the IME sent an appropriate content.
We will re-evaluate the hinting capability on the API if we see enough demand in the future.
Relnote: ""Removed `hintMediaTypes` parameter from `Modifier.contentReceiver`. Developers were already encouraged to check the received `TransferableContent`'s media type for themselves.""

https://github.com/d4rken-org/sdmaid-se.git,6e62b79c5f1dea29a32b1975a7e54455813319a0,"AppCleaner: Improve ACS based cache clearing resilience by detecting storage entries ""on-the-fly"" via app size values
Most ""Storage"" entries in system details have a summary row that says something like ""604 MB in internal storage""
While we don't know the translation of ""in internal storage"", we know the app size, so we can look for ""604 MB"".
There is a slight chance for false-positives if the data-usage of the app is the same as the app size, but that's pretty rare.
If that happens SD Maid just keeps retrying as usual.
With 218 apps this had a 99% success rate.
There was one app (""Digital Wellbeing"", a system app), that showed the wrong app size (36,06 MB) on first load.
Only when SD Maid retried and reloaded the settings details, the size displayed correct (35,96 MB).
No idea why the size was displayed incorrectly the first time...
When clearing caches via ACS we now have 3 tier process:
1: Try to get the storage entry label via string resource ID

https://github.com/duckduckgo/Android.git,23d248737c29091c415e3db55b42fb08f3aa7ed4,"load privacy plugins data into memory just for main process (#4189)
Many privacy plugins data is loaded into memory for both `:app` and `:vpn` processes.
Even when the plugins are only used from the app process, because privacy config is only downloaded from the `main` process, the loading mistakenly happens in the `vpn` process as well because

all plugins have a `loadToMemory` method that is executed in the `init` block (ie. when the instance is created) and the `PluginPoint<MainProcessLifecycleObserver>` is a dependency to the `Application` instance,
which is created for both `vpn` and `app` processes.
However loading into data for the `vpn` process should not be required/done as we don't use that data from the `vpn` process.
This task is a stop gap to avoid that in-memory data loading to happen.
In sub-sequent PRs we'll want to have a better nad more standardize mechanism to perform in-memory Cache backed by disk storage.
Code review and smoke test the app
Make sure privacy config downloads and sets correctly.

Paging logic to convert a stream of LoadHints from RecyclerView bind
calls to PageEvents, which are incremental updates as pages are fetched.
This is implemented the new Pager, PagerState and PageFetcher.
A Pager is a snapshot / single generation representation of the backing
PagedSource. Converts a stream of LoadHints / bind calls from
RecyclerView into PageEvents by making calls to PagedSource.load. The
stream of PageEvents are exposed via PagedData. A Pager lives within a
job that is cancelled whenever the PagedSource is invalidated.
PagerState holds state that must persist across individual resolution of
LoadHints. It must be accessed with a lock within Pager as it is shared
between async loads (start / end can happen at the same time).
PageFetcher converts a stream of refresh tokens, which are sent whenever
a PagedSource is invalidated, into a stream of PagedData. PageFetcher is
multi-generation across generations of PagedData, created using Pager,

https://github.com/androidx/androidx.git,40a9f48c31d460a563d8dd323797822b56b174c8,"Correctly fixup linked list nodes after tombstone cleanup
When a SieveCache contained enough tombstones to require
dropDeletes() to run and if the entries have hash collisions,
the existing code could corrupt its internal mapping of
older indices to new indices post rehash.
Instead of tracking a simple mapping of previous/new index
to be able to fixup the nodes linked list, this change
tracks a more complex mapping consisting of a pair of
(source, destination) indices per entry. When entries are
moved around the arrays, we use the source mapping to go
update the original mapping to properly track where the
value ends up at the end of the rehash.
See comments in dropDeletes() for more details.
The new behavior is validated by a new unit test that
triggers numerous hash collisions. The test fails without
This change also fix the way values were swapped in the arrays

https://github.com/androidx/androidx.git,5dc4f337e67d85dd9d93b5c4ea72256ab0d6449a,"Adds viewport listener and coordinate translation APIs
Optimizes PageLayoutManager to compute certain data that's
derived from the current viewport, namely the visible regions
of visible pages and each visible page's location in view,
only once each time the viewport changes.
Makes visible pages / fully visible pages normal properties
in lieu of observable flows. There's no use-case for observing
these values, only reading their current value. Computing
visible pages and page locations synchronously and directly
when the viewport (scroll, zoom, size) changes enables real-
time dispatching of pagination information to listeners.
Shores up test coverage of PageLayoutManager.
Adds a few clarifying comments related to ""view"" vs ""content""
coordinates and what each means throughout PdfView.
This not only maintains the fast scroll performance
optimizations from aosp/3511737 but improves upon them.

This CL updates the compilation testing infra and refactors the
The biggest change is that now there is an API to assert compilation
In this new API, the handler is responsible to make a failure assertion
if the compilation should fail, otherwise, test will expect compilation
to succeed and fail the test if the compilation fails.
As we add tests in Room, I'll add APIs to also assert generated code but
right now we don't have that use case hence not adding them (also, it is
not clear yet how it will work with kotlin compile testing).
To be able to collect diagnostics before it goes to the compilation
infra, I've moved XMessager to an abstract class (from interface) and
add ability to watch messages. Unfortunately, kotlin compile testing
does not provide an API to get messages with diagnostic types which is
why we have the abstraction on our end.

With chaining being the most common way to combine several modifiers together, chainig also has few other parameters:
2. Chaining looks structured and implies order
Both this points are missing in `Modifier.background.padding + another` case.

Therefore, the proposal is to substitute plus with then to encourage people to type `Modifier.background().padding().then(another)` for better readability and consistency. This was also possible with `padding.plus(another)`, but it appeared to be not enough.
infix stays in then version as well to make it easier to write `fun Modifier.myModifier() = this then MyModifierImpl()`

Relnote: ""Modifier.plus has been deprecated, use Modifier.then instead. 'Then' has a stronger signal of ordering, while also prohibits to type `Modifier.padding().background() + anotherModifier`, which breaks the chain and harder to read""

https://github.com/androidx/androidx.git,acddd8200fef11464c270b852b2af442c1ffc0f8,"Wrote an implementation of R4A that uses the IR
Our old code generation strategy was to introduce extension points that allowed us to poke
at the compiler internals and effectively ""trick"" it into producing roughly what we wanted.  Then
we could go in after the fact and massage the bytecode until we got the desired output.  This
strategy was challenging to write/maintain and very error prone, leading to subtle bugs.
This commit is effectively a rewrite of the entire R4A compiler plugin to use the new IR backend.
This new approach will allow us to utilize the IR pipeline to perform transformations prior to
bytecode generation, which will provide us with a much better foundation for R4A's future.
This code still needs a bunch of cleanup, but I figured it's better to get it merged now
so we everyone else can start experimenting on top of it, rather than to wait until it's
perfect.  I'll go ahead and submit some cleanup diffs soon.

The message explicitly states the old approach was 'challenging to write/maintain' and led to 'subtle bugs', while this change aims to provide a 'better foundation'. This is a structural refactoring to reduce technical debt and improve maintainability.",,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,
https://github.com/androidx/androidx.git,ddc49c903f3d3e853ff6440720699de8f17ee1c9,"Don't return collapsedSemantics on unattached or deactivated nodes

This patch ensures that if a node is deactivated but not attached then `collapsedSemantics` are not returned (and `applySemantics` is not called). This addresses the exception thrown when `applySemantics` is called on an unattached node.

In returning null for deactivated nodes, the SemanticsNodeInteraction.assertIsDeactivated assert needs to be refactored. We need to surface deactivated nodes but cannot do so with testTags because testTags come from semantics config, which comes from modifier nodes. This patch rewrites all foundation tests using the `assertIsDeactivated` assert by instead saving the semantics ids of relevant nodes, then verifying that those nodes all are deactivated by checking `layoutInfo.isDeactivated`.

This lays the foundation to multiple future works, e.g. we need to check if use case combination is guaranteed to be supported or unsupported through the current CameraX logic before checking with feature combination query APIs. Since CameraX Feature Combination Query API needs to be workable before binding flow, we needed to move this logic out of CameraUseCaseAdapter since CameraUseCaseAdapter is created during the binding flow. Besides, design-wise, it makes more sense to check if use case combination is supported in a CameraInfo or separate class, not CameraUseCaseAdapter.

Note that this CL does not focus on doing a full refactoring of the use case combination supported logic. Rather, it just moves the current logic to dedicated/appropriate classes without changing any logic flow.

To do direct boot, important files need to be moved to device storage (i.e. not as safe storage). The only thing sensitive is user credentials (server settings in each profile) and the config file passed to libss-*.so. In order to implement direct boot, at least the active profile has to be moved/copied to device storage.

This commit keeps profile information (and temporary data used by ProfileConfigFragment) and Shadowsocks config in credential storage and move everything else to device storage. In order to make direct boot work, active profile is also copied into device storage and Shadowsocks config file is created in device storage during direct boot. Cleanup of those information is also implemented.
* `android:directBootAware` can't be used on application element. That seems to be used by system apps.
* Old GSM files are not migrated (see App.kt) to device storage.

This change provides a full implementation of the VirtualSessionState
object which is used to create the CameraCaptureSession.
At the most basic level VirtualSessionState is responsible for waiting
until it has a valid CameraDevice and a valid set of Surfaces available
to use in order to create the CaptureSession. Once the CaptureSession is
configured, a RequestProcessor instance is created and attached to the
GraphProcessor, which can then submit any currently queued requests.
This change also includes GraphState, which is responsible for opening
the camera and wiring it to the VirtualSessionState when start is called
and for detaching them when stop or close is called.

This change moves AutoCloser and relevant classes to Coroutines, aligning with the rest of the Coroutine-based infra refactor for KMP. It also reduces the lock contention by using atomics and avoiding the usage of the lock object along with improving the auto-boxing of binding arguments in the AutoClosingSQLiteStatement.

This CL also adds an internal 'Watch' interface to get the uptime in millis for time tracking, enabling time movement using a fake watch for tests. By using a test watch along with a TestCoroutineDispatcher, this CL fixes the flaky tests that have plague auto-close for a while and removes the usages of Thread.sleep(), thus creating deterministic tests.

,EnergySmell,qwen3.5:122b,ConcurrencySmell,The commit explicitly migrates AutoCloser from a lock-based implementation to an atomic/executor-based approach, replacing `synchronized(lock)` with `Handler` and `Executor` for scheduling auto-close operations. It also addresses flaky tests caused by race conditions in time-dependent logic by introducing a testable 'Watch' interface and removing non-deterministic `Thread.sleep()` calls, directly targeting concurrency issues.",,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,

https://github.com/androidx/androidx.git,1b849a6d08abfdf0adc73ebedbfa2d4932d01e5e,"Fix selection of text that has maxLines configured.

If a Text composable overflows due to defined maxLines and it's made selectable, selecting the text causes a crash if the handle is moved outside text boundaries. The reason is that TextLayoutResult can still return the position of non-rendered characters when queried by the gesture handlers.

Instead of modifying selection behavior, this change limits where the selection handles can be drawn by adding a calculated `lastVisibleOffset` value. Selection handles are always coerced between 0 and `lastVisibleOffset` to make sure that handles are never tried to be drawn where invisible (overflown) characters were supposed to be placed.

Create an API for performing database transaction with coroutines. When
starting a transaction coroutine a thread of the database query executor
is hold for performing all database operations within the coroutine. It
is recommended that all DAO functions invoked are also suspend
functions. In the case they are blocking DAO methods, then invoking
those on a different context other than the transaction context will
throw an exception, preventing possible deadlocks.

,ConcurrencySmell,qwen3.5:122b,ConcurrencySmell,The commit introduces a new API for performing database transactions with coroutines (`withTransaction`), explicitly addressing thread-safety and deadlock prevention. Originally we tried to use the auto redrawing based on RenderNode's elevation which proved to be tricky to support and caused a number of bugs. Instead of relaying on this mechanism we decided to decouple shadows size from z index as features and this CL is a first part of it. Here we still use DrawLayerModifier's elevation as a zIndex, but we do the drawing reordering manually.
This should also improve the drawing performance as we invalidate less(on apis < 29) and do expensive enableZ calls less often.
